item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While still asleep, hosts realize and understand that they are dreaming, but also an understanding that they are entirely safe.

I get what you were trying to say, but I don't get why you chose to word it like that. It's grammatically incorrect and confusing. I would fix it but I think the sentence should be rewritten to be more clear and that seems like a job for the author.

I like the general idea, but the skip seems a bit feeble and those grammatical mistakes are too much for me, I'll downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this a nightmare that eats other nightmares? Literally an apex predator of nightmares that eats all the others?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … The initial patient inducted in as D class. For their troubles, for the crime of whatever it was in the hospital for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think of this SCP either as a former patient that died and decided to fuck some shit up, or as a nightmare that grew semi-sentient, cannibalistic, and decided to start preying on its former brethren.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awww, it's a nightmare-hopping dream-friend who stops the nightmares for you. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Spooky +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. The more you know.

Oh damn, that's terrifying, isn't it? That someone would endure D-Class duty for a crime that is minor, or something we wouldn't even consider to be a crime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most people under a death sentence are Chinese (more than all other countries in the world put together). China does execute for nonviolent crimes like fraud and drug possession. They also execute for inchoate offenses, in other words attempted crimes where the crime wasn't successfully committed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But that's just it: it cannot jump from its host to anyone else unless you specifically put another person there and have them sleep within 100 meters of each other. It doesn't matter where it gravitates to, it's specifically got a 100 meter jumping ability. Based on the rules you've set up regarding this SCP, the containment procedures don't make sense. Now, maybe, since this thing "eats" nightmares, it needs to feed or it will starve to death? But that needs to be included.

Now, the concept of a beneficial creature who wants to protect people from nightmares, or just happens to have bad dreams as its natural food source, has potential as an idea. The thing I think is missing, and what will probably help once you get that down, is a sense of tone. This creature is inherently the opposite of scary - it's a thing that helps make scary things go away - so what do you want the audience to feel if not fear?

I think once you get that down, the rest will flow from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair point to make, and of course whether or not D-Class personnel actually are from death row depends on the person telling the story.

But this still doesn't remedy the problem. "Nonviolent" is vague - does it mean they never committed a violent crime, or that they're cooperative with Foundation personnel - and also seems to be an unnecessary requirement. What does it matter if the guy's violent or not? Again, this thing can't leave the host unless there's someone else sleeping within 100 meters. As long as the host isn't suicidal (what would happen if the host dies and no one's within 100 meters? Would it die like demons in the movie Fallen?) it's unnecessary to keep anyone else in there with him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While its a headcanon-to-headcanon thing, I don't think all D-Class are violent criminals. Hell, in some SCPs you can't have violent criminals dealing with the object.

Non-violent D-Class are just people the Foundation has acquired or demoted to the role, whether criminial or not. I can't really tell you to cancel downvote for that (as you have more reasons than just that to downvote) but frankly its not unheard of and opens up the opportunity for some D-Class to be random people abducted by the Men In Black and experimented with. Which is a twist on the classic MIB urban legend, which fits with the basic idea of the Foundation (and is exactly what I use as a feature in my articles).

Using three people allows it to cycle between the three in a predictable pattern, allowing for regular tests as it moves from an 'empty' host to a "full" one.

Say Host A has held the SCP for a few days, and is having clean, restful dreams each night because of it. Meanwhile, Host B hasn't held 2840 for two weeks now, and is beginning to have her regular terrors again. So they can then guess when 2840 will jump to Host B, measuring the intensity of the dreams that it seems to be attracted to. Host C has begun to have unpleasant dreams as well, but won't be quite 'tasty' enough to attract 2840 for a while, allowing them to study Host B while Host C's condition continues returning to its baseline, and Host A is allowed a brief reprieve from his nightmares after 2840 'cleansed' him.

Otherwise, yes, it is rather awkward in parts, thats a weakness of my own. I'm going to update it soon. EVERYONE has told me its missing something, I need to find what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, "nonviolent D-class" sent out a red flag. D-Class are, at least allegedly, criminals on death row.

Read Security Clearance Levels. It's pretty explicit that you can have non-violent D-Class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First of all, "nonviolent D-class" sent out a red flag. D-Class are, at least allegedly, criminals on death row.

Given the number of people on death row and the publicity they get, as long as the diversity in both the general population and people on death row even, there's always the possibility for nonviolent D-Class. I mean, I've written one myself. It may depend on the source and their background, and I know you said "allegedly", but it's still something to point out :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are several problems with the writing. It's awkward as a general rule, but a few things stick out.

First of all, "nonviolent D-class" sent out a red flag. D-Class are, at least allegedly, criminals on death row. "Are permitted entertainment material as requested" is vague, and sounds as though they can have anything they want without limitation. "While still asleep, hosts realize and understand that they are dreaming, but also an understanding that they are entirely safe," is grammatically incorrect.

But also the Special Containment Procedures don't make sense. It's a thing that cures night terrors and wants to protect people from nightmares (sort of like Snoozee if he actually did his damn job). But why do we need three people to contain this? It can't leave if no one's sleeping within 100 meters of it, it's dormant when the person isn't asleep, and seems to be largely benevolent. There's no reason to use more than one person to contain it.

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept here is very cute. I really like the concept, but I can't bring myself to upvote it because the writing doesn't feel up to par. There's a lot of tone issues and other hiccups, and there's enough of them that it really harms the overall quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
<scratches head> Mmmh. Why non-violent D-class?
And why contain this skip, as a matter of fact? It's doing good all around! I don't see any negative effect that would justify a containment… Why not let it roam freely? A frightful number of Foundation personnel would surely be happy to have their bad dreams neutralized!
(OTOH, I Wonder what could happen if SCP-231… End of the world or neutralized?)
(And 682? Perhaps it's mean because of bad dreams…?)
Er…. sorry… thinking aloud…

Otherwise, a pleasant, resting, well-written SCP. Upvote, I think!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing the second and third paragraphs completely- finally someone managed to put into words my general feelings about dream SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no issue with the writing itself, and I like the core idea of an entity that hunts, consumes, and replaces negative dream imagery. That could go in some pretty wild places.

Dream-based articles are difficult to write in my experience, especially in a manner that's intended to be an informative document for an organization's records. Dream imagery is amorphous and impenetrable. This makes it ideal for experimental prose and surrealistic exercises, but it also means that it's very difficult to write something relating the experiences of another person, much less a common experience between multiple people.

I won't say that something like this can't work in the SCP format, but I do think the documentation needs to delve a little more deeply (I would prefer much deeper, personally) into what this thing is and what it does to people for it to really take advantage of the starting concept. There's the beginnings of something really good here, but I don't think it's there yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never liked dream skips, and this one is no different. (The reason why is there's no way to really know what they're doing if you're not the one dreaming.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • I like how this SCP tries to use fear-inducing images that instil a sense of safety. it's odd and quirky, and something made in dreams.
  • It's interesting to see that it just wants to remove phobias.

Cons:

  • The only nitpick is that the "individuals will remember their dreams each night, though imperfectly and forgetting minor details." could be summed up as lucid dreaming.

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoop posted the wrong draft. Adjusted by moving a small paragraph to its proper place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this. There were a few grammatical errors but they were minor enough to not have an effect on my vote.

Specifically, I like that this is an example of the Foundation containing something that isn't necessarily a horrible monster out to destroy us all, but still poses a threat to the consensus.

item.title: New SCP
item.content:encoded:
Not sure where this post originally went, but here's my short SCP contest entry. I'm rather rusty.

Thank you to everybody who helped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted this, but it barely passed my upvote threshold. That's not to say there's something wrong with it, but rather that the idea is just cute enough. There's not much writing to judge here other than the idea.
